event: Neolithic communities at sites such as Ain Ghazal and Beidha establish some of the world's earliest settled farming villages.
The large Neolithic settlement of Ain Ghazal near Amman flourishes, producing the famous plaster human statues among the oldest known.
Early Bronze Age towns with fortifications and copper mining develop across the Transjordan region.
Amorite and Canaanite peoples settle the region, integrating it into the trade networks of the ancient Near East.
The Iron Age kingdoms of Ammon, Moab, and Edom emerge in the lands east of the Jordan River.
King Mesha of Moab erects the Mesha Stele, recording Moab's revolt against the Kingdom of Israel.
The Assyrian Empire under Tiglath-Pileser III brings Ammon, Moab, and Edom under its dominance as tributary states.
The Neo-Babylonian Empire absorbs the region following its conquests across the Levant.
The Achaemenid Persian Empire incorporates the Transjordan lands after the fall of Babylon.
Alexander the Great's conquests bring the region into the Hellenistic world, spreading Greek culture and city life.
The Nabataeans establish Petra as the capital of their trading kingdom, controlling lucrative incense and spice routes.
Roman general Pompey conquers the region, and Greek cities form the Decapolis league under Roman influence.
Emperor Trajan annexes the Nabataean Kingdom, creating the Roman province of Arabia Petraea with Petra and Bosra.
The region becomes part of the Christian Byzantine Empire, and churches and mosaics flourish in cities like Madaba.
Sasanian Persian forces briefly overrun the Byzantine provinces of the region during their wars with Constantinople.
The Muslim victory at the Battle of Yarmouk decisively ends Byzantine rule and brings the region into the Islamic caliphate.
Under the Umayyad Caliphate the region prospers, dotted with desert castles such as Qasr Amra and Qasr Kharana.
The Abbasid Caliphate takes power, shifting the empire's center to Baghdad and reducing the region's prominence.
Crusaders build the castles of Montreal (Shobak) and later Kerak to control the lands east of the Jordan River.
Saladin's Ayyubid forces capture the Crusader castle of Kerak, consolidating Muslim control of the region.
The Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt takes control after halting the Mongol advance at the Battle of Ain Jalut.
The Ottoman Empire conquers the region following its victory over the Mamluks, beginning four centuries of rule.
Construction of the Hejaz Railway begins, linking Damascus through Transjordan toward Medina.
The Arab Revolt against Ottoman rule, led by Sharif Hussein and his sons, sweeps across the region with British support.
Arab and Allied forces under Emir Faisal liberate the region from Ottoman control at the close of World War I.
Britain establishes the Emirate of Transjordan under Emir Abdullah I as a separate entity within its Palestine Mandate.
An Anglo-Transjordanian agreement grants the emirate greater autonomy while Britain retains control of finance and foreign affairs.
Transjordan gains full independence from Britain, and Abdullah I is proclaimed king of the new Hashemite kingdom.
Jordan participates in the first Arab-Israeli war following the establishment of Israel, occupying the West Bank and East Jerusalem.
The country is renamed the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan and signs an armistice with Israel.
Jordan formally annexes the West Bank and East Jerusalem, extending citizenship to their Palestinian residents.
King Abdullah I is assassinated in Jerusalem, and his son Talal briefly succeeds before abdicating for health reasons.
A new constitution is adopted establishing a constitutional monarchy with an elected parliament.
Hussein bin Talal is enthroned as king, beginning a reign that would last nearly half a century.
Jordan is admitted to the United Nations as a member state.
King Hussein dismisses British officer Glubb Pasha and ends the Anglo-Jordanian treaty, asserting national independence.
Jordan loses the West Bank and East Jerusalem to Israel in the Six-Day War, sending a wave of refugees east of the river.
The Black September conflict erupts as the Jordanian army clashes with Palestinian guerrilla factions, expelling them from the country.
King Hussein renounces Jordan's legal and administrative claims to the West Bank in favor of the Palestine Liberation Organization.
Jordan holds its first parliamentary elections in over two decades, beginning a process of political liberalization.
Jordan signs a peace treaty with Israel, becoming the second Arab state to normalize relations.
King Hussein dies after 46 years on the throne, and his son Abdullah II succeeds him.
Jordan joins the World Trade Organization, accelerating economic reforms and free-trade agreements.
Coordinated suicide bombings strike hotels in Amman, killing dozens and prompting a national security response.
Arab Spring protests prompt King Abdullah II to dismiss governments and pledge political and economic reforms.
Jordan, hosting hundreds of thousands of Syrian refugees, faces deepening economic strain amid the regional crisis.
Authorities announce the foiling of an alleged plot involving former Crown Prince Hamzah, exposing tensions within the royal family.
